item.title: Garden City P&Z Commissioner Starr Shepard running for city council
item.description: A Garden City Planning & Zoning Commissioner is hoping to get promoted. Starr Shepard, a member of the small city’s P&Z commission for the past three and a half years, is running for one of the two seats up for grabs on the Garden City City Council in November. If elected, she hopes to find ways to bring in more grant funds to improve sidewalk infrastructure, implement incentives to encourage affordable housing development, make the city’s zoning laws user-friendly, and provide more representation for residents on the city’s more urban east end. “I’m running for a local office because I believe in the power of community and our local government shapes our daily lives, how we move and how we live, but it’s not just about policies and it’s about people and listening to their concerns and acting on their ideas so we can work together to create a city where everyone has the opportunity to be seen and heard,” Shepard said. She is one of six candidates competing for the seats, with the top two vote getters landing a spot on the dais. Shepard is running against Kelceymarie Warner, Dennis Mansfield, incumbent City Council Member Teresa Jorgensen, Wendy Carver-Herbert, and Gage Coprivnicar. Shephard is currently a workplace investigator with J.R. Simplot after stints running community health programs on youth suicide prevention, substance abuse, and mental healthcare for frontline workers during the COVID-19 pandemic with BPA Health. She also previously worked for twenty years assisting immigrant women who were victims of domestic violence and sexual assault, as well as creating training programs for law enforcement to recognize and investigate human trafficking. Shepard also worked in Guatemala on economic development programs for Mayan women after the long-running civil war in the country ended. She graduated from Virginia Commonwealth University with a degree in Psychology. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Shepard said her nearly four years on the city’s P&Z commission give her a depth of insight into how growth impacts neighborhoods and how the city handles growth currently. She said her biggest priority when looking at how the city is writing zoning code changes and developing land use policies is ensuring that codes are easy to interpret. Shepard pointed to the city’s recent zoning changes, which upzoned some areas of the city and downzoned others, as an example of how the city should be changing its code so the rules are less subjective and keep pace with growth in the area. For example, Garden City repealed its Live Work Create District in 2022, promoting mixed-use development in the East End, after a court challenge found aspects of the district’s requirements subjective. Shepard said Garden City needs more housing to address the region’s shortage of homes, but after recent changes, she doesn’t think the city’s comprehensive plans and zoning should be changed further unless there is extensive public input for a new comprehensive plan. She does support more mixed-use and transit-oriented development for the land-locked city as it continues to grow, along with balancing the needs of current residents. “I believe people should feel secure in their right to use their property and that means making zoning and land use decisions clear and consistent and avoiding rules that make it harder for small businesses and families to use their properties,” she said. Another focus for Shepard is ensuring that residents of the east end, which historically has been home to most of the city’s mobile home parks and is now rapidly redeveloping, have representation on the city council. Currently, all members of the Garden City Council reside in the city’s more suburban west end. She’d propose a monthly town hall for all residents to share concerns and thoughts with city officials to increase connection with the community. “I’d like to use my experience on P&Z to work with city officials, developers, residents, neighboring cities, partner agencies, and different organizations to ensure open communication because if we can coordinate with surrounding governments and groups to better guide growth so it’s a sustainable balance for the whole community,” she said. “My whole thing is to build consensus so neighbors have a place where they can speak and have their voices heard.” Adding more infrastructure for pedestrians and cyclists, especially in the east end, is a priority for Shepard. She noted the piecemeal nature of sidewalk construction in the rapidly developing area, which leaves blocks with inconsistent sidewalk coverage. Sidewalks, bike lanes, and road infrastructure are governed by the Ada County Highway District, but Shepard said she would like to learn how Garden City could press for more improvements in collaboration with the transportation agency. “We have a lot of pedestrians, especially around 34th street, and I’ve seen people down there navigating pushing baby carriers and navigating cars and bikes and there are so many people down there that we need to have sidewalks,” she said. “…Right now we get sidewalks when we get a development agreement, so sidewalks are piecemeal, but if we can work to maybe make impact fees go into a fund so that one year this whole street gets done, so pretty soon all the streets have sidewalks, I think that would bring a lot to our community and that would help businesses because people would feel more comfortable walking down here and it’s safer.” She would also like for the city to consider hiring staff to be grant writers, contingent on finding funding to pay for their positions, tasked with helping the city secure federal funds to help build projects in Garden City that are out of reach of the small city’s budget. She cited recent improvements ACHD is planning, like an enhanced pedestrian crossing on Chinden Boulevard, funded by a grant from the federal government as an example.
